phrasalVerbs.definition: To accept responsibility, a task, or hire new staff.
examples: She took on an extra shift to earn more money.
The firm is taking on ten interns this summer.
Don't take on more work than you can handle.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To have a friendly relationship with someone.
examples: I get along really well with my new team.
It's hard to get along with people who never listen.
Do your kids get along well at school?

phrasalVerbs.definition: To understand or find the answer to something.
examples: Can you figure out what's wrong with the printer?
I finally figured out how to use this app.
We need to figure out our next steps quickly.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To register arrival at a hotel, airport, or event.
examples: We checked in to the hotel at 2 PM.
Please check in before boarding the plane.
You should check in 90 minutes before the flight.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To think of or produce an idea or solution.
examples: The team came up with a brilliant new idea.
Can you come up with a better plan?
Who came up with this recipe? It's delicious!

phrasalVerbs.definition: To take care of someone or something.
examples: Could you look after my plants while I'm away?
She looks after three children under the age of five.
It's important to look after your health.

fillInExercises.sentence: We need to ___ a solution to this problem before tomorrow's meeting.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Come up with' means to think of or produce an idea or solution.

fillInExercises.sentence: Could you ___ after my dog this weekend? I'm going away.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Look after' means to care for someone or something.

fillInExercises.sentence: He's great at his job because he ___ well with everyone.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Get along' means to have a good relationship.

fillInExercises.sentence: Guests are expected to ___ in before 12 noon on the day of departure.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Check in' means to register at a hotel or airport.

fillInExercises.sentence: The department will ___ five new members of staff next month.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Take on' means to hire or accept new responsibilities.

fillInExercises.sentence: She couldn't ___ out why the project had failed.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Figure out' means to understand or find the reason for something.

fillInExercises.sentence: Can you ___ my bike while I'm in hospital? I don't want it stolen.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Look after' means to keep something safe and in good condition.

fillInExercises.sentence: The engineer quickly ___ up with a temporary fix for the broken pipe.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Come up with' (past: came up with) means to produce a solution.

lines.speaker: Nadia
lines.text: I still can't [figure out] why the client rejected our proposal.

lines.speaker: Tom
lines.text: Maybe we need to [come up with] a completely different approach?

lines.speaker: Nadia
lines.text: I agree. We should also [take on] someone with experience in this area.

lines.speaker: Tom
lines.text: I'll [look after] the existing clients so they don't worry in the meantime.

lines.speaker: Nadia
lines.text: Perfect. Let's [check in] with the director tomorrow morning.

lines.speaker: Tom
lines.text: If we [get along] well with him, I'm sure we'll find a way forward.

lines.speaker: Sam
lines.text: We need to [check in] to the hotel by 4. Should we set off now?

lines.speaker: Jess
lines.text: In a second. I'm trying to [figure out] the best route.

lines.speaker: Sam
lines.text: Good thinking. Can you [look after] this bag while I go to the shop?

lines.speaker: Jess
lines.text: Of course. Did you [come up with] a plan for the weekend?

lines.speaker: Sam
lines.text: Yes! And I really [get along] well with the family who runs the guesthouse.

lines.speaker: Jess
lines.text: Lovely! I'm happy to [take on] the role of tour guide for Saturday.
